data.title: Microsoft strikes record soil carbon credit deal
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) has signed a 12-year agreement with Indigo Carbon to buy a record 2.85M soil carbon removal credits tied to regenerative agriculture in the U.S., as the tech firm aims to become carbon negative by 2030.

As Indigo and Microsoft's third transaction, this deal builds on prior purchases of 40,000 tons in 2024 and 60,000 tons in 2025.

Under the agreement, Microsoft will purchase credits generated under the Carbon by Indigo program, Indigo's U.S.-based project, emphasizing regenerative agriculture's role in high-integrity carbon removals.

Regenerative agriculture practices have been recognized by governments and climate organizations as a valuable tool for carbon removal, with the ability to remove more than 3.5 gigatons of carbon-dioxide equivalent every year in addition to improving soil health, increasing crop resiliency and yields, and scaling local investment.

While Microsoft did not disclose the transaction details, a person with knowledge of the deal told Reuters that it is valued between $171M and $228M.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company hikes dividend by 20% to NT$6.00
data.description:
• Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) declares NT$6.00/share quarterly dividend, 20% increase from prior dividend of NT$5.00.
  • Payable April 9; for shareholders of record March 23; ex-div March 17.
  • See TSM Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Toyota Industries jumps to a record high after Toyota Motor's sweetened buyout offer to over $35B
data.description:
Toyota Industries (TYIDY) (TYIDF) share price surged on the Japanese stock exchange to a record high after Toyota Motors (TM) announced a sweetened buyout offer by over 15% to over $35B.

Toyota Motor raised the buyout offer for the group company to 18,800 yen per share, from 16,300 yen apiece announced in June last year.

Toyota Industries, the original founder of Toyota Motor in the 1930s, produces forklifts, engines, and auto parts.

data.title: Earnings Snapshot: TSMC Q4 revenue, earnings jump as company beats estimates
Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Non-GAAP EPS of $3.14 beats by $0.16, revenue of $33.73B beats by $1B
data.description:
• Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company press release (TSM): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $3.14 beats by $0.16.
  • Revenue of $33.73B (+25.5% Y/Y)  beats by $1B.
  • In the fourth quarter, shipments of 3-nanometer accounted for 28% of total wafer revenue; 5-nanometer accounted for 35%; 7-nanometer accounted for 14%. Advanced technologies, defined as 7-nanometer and more advanced technologies, accounted for 77% of total wafer revenue.

data.title: Cogeco reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Cogeco Inc. press release (CGO:CA): Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $3.00.
  • Revenue of $735.6M (-3.8% Y/Y).

data.title: OpenAI gets back senior executives from Mira Murati’s Thinking Machines Lab
data.description:
Mira Murati, a former OpenAI (OPENAI) CTO, recently said that her startup—Thinking Machines Lab—will say goodbye to two of its co-founders, both of whom are headed back to OpenAI.

Barret Zoph, the co-founder and CTO, and Luke Metz, another co-founder of Thinking Machines Lab, are returning to the ChatGPT maker.

Both previously worked at OpenAI (OPENAI) before joining Murati to launch Thinking Machines Lab in late 2024. Sam Schoenholz, a senior researcher, is also heading back. Murati announced Zoph's departure on X, naming Soumith Chintala as the new CTO without mentioning Metz.

Thinking Machines Lab raised $2B in a seed round last July at a $12B valuation from investors like Andreessen Horowitz, Nvidia (NVDA), and AMD (AMD).

data.title: U.S. completes first sale of Venezuelan oil, valued at $500 million - report
data.description:
• The U.S. has completed the first sales of Venezuelan oil that are part of a $2 billion deal reached earlier this month between Caracas and Washington, a U.S. official told Reuters on Wednesday.
  • Additional sales are expected in the coming days and weeks, the official added.
  • The main account is located in Qatar, according to an industry source familiar with the plan, who said the country represented a neutral venue where funds can move with U.S. approval and without risk of seizure.
  • Chevron (CVX) meanwhile, likely will be one of several U.S. companies to secure approvals from the Trump administration to conduct business in Venezuela as oil companies, according to another report, which also said Marathon Petroleum (MPC) is in talks with U.S. officials to receive Venezuelan crude for its refineries and that Valero Energy (VLO) and global traders Glencore (GLCNF) (GLNCY) and Mercuria also have been in discussions for licenses to do business with Venezuela.

data.title: Caledonia Mining prices $125M convertible debt offering
data.description:
Caledonia Mining (CMCL) priced its private offering of $125M aggregate principal amount of 5.875% convertible senior notes due 2033.

The size of the convertible notes offering was increased from the previously announced $100M aggregate principal amount of notes. In addition, Caledonia granted the initial purchasers of the notes a 13-day option to purchase up to an additional $25M of the notes.

The notes will accrue interest payable semiannually in arrears at 5.875% per year and mature on January 15, 2033, unless earlier converted, redeemed, or repurchased.

Each $1,000 note can be converted into 24.6837 common shares, implying a conversion price of about $40.51 per share. This is roughly a 25% premium to the stock’s last closing price on the NYSE American on January 14, 2026.

The convertible notes offering is expected to close on January 20, 2026, subject to the satisfaction of customary closing conditions.

Caledonia expects net proceeds of about $120.2M from the offering, or about $144.4M if the underwriters buy the full additional allotment, after fees and expenses. The company plans to use the proceeds to fund roughly $12M of capped call transactions (or $14.4M if the option is fully exercised) and for general financial flexibility and other potential uses.

Source: Press Release

data.title: Bank executives push back against Trump’s call for credit card interest rate cap
data.description:
Executives from banks, including Citigroup (C) and JPMorgan Chase (JPM), were critical of U.S. President Donald Trump’s call for a one-year cap on credit card interest rates.

"Effective January 20, 2026, I, as President of the United States, am calling for a one year cap on credit card interest rates of 10%," Trump posted on Truth Social on Jan. 9.

Trump did not elaborate on how the plan will be executed and banks will be made to comply.

Citigroup CEO Jane Fraser, however, said that the cap would have a "severe impact on access to credit and on consumer spending across the country."

Speaking at Citi's post-earnings conference call, Fraser said that "If you make these products unprofitable, that spending will be drastically reduced."

"A vast majority of consumers and businesses will lose access to credit cards … They'd be forced to pursue more predatory alternatives. And you'd only be left with the wealthy having access to credit cards, and nobody wants that," she added.

JP Morgan CEO James Dimon also said that an imposition of price controls would "compress the profit margins" and "the provision of the service will change dramatically."

"Specifically, people will lose access to credit, like on a very, very extensive and broad basis, especially the people who need it the most, honestly. And so that's a pretty severely negative consequence for consumers and frankly, probably also a negative consequence for the economy as a whole right now," Dimon said.

"If you bring the caps down, you're going to constrict credit, meaning less people will get credit cards and the balance available to them on those credit cards will also be restricted. And so you have to balance that against what you're trying to achieve from the affordability," Bank of America (BAC) CEO Brian Moynihan said in the company’s earnings call.

A joint statement from the Consumer Bankers Association, Bank Policy Institute, American Bankers Association, Financial Services Forum and Independent Community Bankers of America also pushed back against a 10% rate cap.

The statement said that the cap would "reduce credit availability" and " drive consumers toward less regulated, more costly alternatives."
